text: This is a presentation on endoscopic closure of a diverticular-related colovesicular fistula.
Eric Pauly has the following disclosures. The other authors have no disclosures.
This is a technique demonstration of endoscopic therapy and definitive closure of a culovesicular fistula following the principles of source control.
The procedure highlights the effectiveness of having both the presence of urological expertise in cystoscopy and technical skills in surgical endoscopy available for management of complex defects.
This case also focuses on the importance of having alternative treatment modalities for patients who are too high risk for surgery.

Patient is a fifty-nine-year-old male with history of diverticulosis, hypertension, and hepatitis C cirrhosis with a MELD score of sixteen who completed treatment prior to intervention. He presented with complaints of fecal urea and pneumaturia.
The colonoscopy reveals sigmoid diverticulosis with visible areas of granulation tissue, but the fistula is not localized.
The CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals a colovesicular fistula located between the anterior bladder dome and the sigmoid colon.
With confirmed clinical and radiographical diagnosis, patient would undergo a combined procedure in the operating room.
The patient was instructed to maintain a clear liquid diet for forty-eight hours prior to the procedure. He also received flea enemolens the evening before and again the morning of the procedure.
Given the patient's cirrhosis and poor MELD score, he underwent monitored anesthesia care with propofol to avoid the risk of general anesthesia.
The urology team performs cystoscopy, visually identifying the fistula tract and cannulating it with a wire.
During minimally invasive surgery, a flexible sigmoidoscope is used to visualize the colovesicular fistula, which is then closed with an over-the-scope clip.
During panendoscopy, the surgeon visualizes a fistula tract located to the left of the midline, extending towards the dome of the bladder.
An angled guide wire is advanced through an open-ended catheter in an attempt to cannulate the fistula tract, with the wire visibly navigating towards the targeted area.
The surgical team switches to a different scope due to difficulty reaching the colon.
A rigid bridge is used as a guide to manipulate a curved catheter into the fistula tract, followed by the insertion of a hydrophilic guide wire through the catheter.
The surgeon advances the catheter further, confirming its placement within the colon using fluoroscopy imaging.
The surgeon advances the guide wire into the colon, following the previously identified fistula tract towards the dome of the bladder.
A flexible gastroscope is advanced into the colon, where the wire is visually identified, grasped, and extracted through the rectum.
The surgeon backloads a GIF-II-T scope with a XII-VI-T type over-the-scope clip affixed to its end, preparing for further intervention within the colon.
The surgeon advances the catheter over the guide wire to reach the level of the fistula within the colon.
Using a combination of wire pull and colon manipulation, the surgeon positions the scope directly facing the defect within the colon.
Aggressive suction is applied, drawing the wall of the colon into the cap of the endoscope.
The surgeon removes the wire and fires the over-the-scope clip, which is visibly positioned well, achieving apparent closure of the endoscopic defect in the colon.
Fluoroscopic evaluation is performed using a catheter in the bladder, showing contrast reaching the clip without passing through, confirming the closure of the endoscopic defect.
The surgeon ablates the fistula on the bladder side using a holmium laser, with visible laser application to the tissue. A Foley catheter is placed to facilitate bladder drainage.
Patient tolerated the procedure without issues and was discharged home on the same day with a Foley catheter.
Medical optimization of the patient's liver disease continued in parallel to our intervention.
He was seen in urology clinic post-op day eight where the Foley was removed without issues.
The patient reported return of fecal urea and pneumaturia three days after catheter removal, and subsequent CT scan demonstrated recurrence of the fistula.
Fortunately, his MELD score had improved from sixteen to nine, allowing him to become a better surgical candidate.
Patient underwent a robotic-assisted sigmoidectomy and has been without issues at one month.

This case highlights and teaches the steps necessary to identify the diverticular fistula, manipulate the colon with a wire, and secure a clip on the defect. The ability to have a wire present to visualize and pull and rotate the colon shows how you can change the angle of the colon to maneuver the defect into view. This was all performed while maintaining the principles of source control.

Due to the patient's cirrhosis and initial MELD score, an endoscopic intervention was the most appropriate alternative at the time. While we did not achieve long-term success, we were able to help bridge a patient with a high-risk MEL score to surgical therapy.
Finally, the assistance from other specialties demonstrates the benefits of a multidisciplinary approach to address complex pathologies.
